constraints: NNN people stand in line to enter your store. Some of them are priority customers, though you don't know which ones yet. Their initial order is [1,2,3,‚Ä¶,N][1, 2, 3, \ldots, N][1,2,3,‚Ä¶,N].
You can change the order of people by doing the following:
Call a single person xxx (1‚Č§x‚Č§N1 \leq x \leq N1‚Č§x‚Č§N). This results in the following change:
If xxx is a priority customer, they will move directly to the front of the line.
If xxx is not a priority customer,
xxx will first move to the front of the line.
Then, as long as the person standing immediately behind xxx is a priority customer, xxx will swap positions with them.
For example, consider P=[1,2,3,4]P = [1, 2, 3, 4]P=[1,2,3,4] with 222 and 444 being priority customers.
If 333 is called, the line becomes [3,1,2,4][3, 1, 2, 4][3,1,2,4].
333 will move to the front of the line; and there are no priority customers immediately behind xxx, so no swaps happen.
If 444 is called next, the line becomes [4,3,1,2][4, 3, 1, 2][4,3,1,2].
444 is a priority customer and so just goes to the front.
If 111 is called next, the line becomes [4,1,3,2][4, 1, 3, 2][4,1,3,2].
111 first goes to the front, and then swaps with 444 who is a priority customer.
If 222 is called now, the line becomes [2,4,1,3][2, 4, 1, 3][2,4,1,3].
If 333 is called, the line becomes [2,4,3,1][2, 4, 3, 1][2,4,3,1].
You're given the final order of people in the line. How many distinct subsets of people could possibly have been priority customers given this final order?

A subset of people could have been priority customers if there's a sequence of calls that results in the given final order, starting from [1,2,3,‚Ä¶,N][1, 2, 3, \ldots, N][1,2,3,‚Ä¶,N] and following the rules.
Note that it's allowed for people to be called any number of times, including 000 or more than 111: the only requirement is that a finite-length sequence of calls results in the given final order.
The answer may be large, so compute it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
Input Format
The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚Äď the number of people.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,‚Ä¶,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1‚Äč,P2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,PN‚Äč.
Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of possible subsets of priority customers, modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
1‚Č§N‚Č§2‚čÖ1051 \leq N \leq 2\cdot 10^51‚Č§N‚Č§2‚čÖ105
1‚Č§Pi‚Č§N1 \leq P_i \leq N1‚Č§Pi‚Äč‚Č§N
Pi‚Č†PjP_i \neq P_jPi‚ÄčÓÄ†=Pj‚Äč for i‚Č†ji \neq jiÓÄ†=j
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚čÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚čÖ105.
